ghi_record.context: Led procurement and logistics for a mid-size medical device distributor operating across East Africa. Managed supplier relationships spanning four countries during two major disruptions: the 2020 global supply chain crisis and the 2021 Suez Canal blockage. Operated with a lean team of six in Nairobi with regional partners in Kampala, Dar es Salaam, and Addis Ababa.

perspective_data.distinctive_lens: Most supply chain managers in the region optimized for cost per unit. I optimized for relationship durability — the ability of a supplier partnership to survive a shock without renegotiation. This came from watching three competitors lose their entire supplier base in 2020 because their contracts had no flexibility built in. I treated every supplier agreement as a stress test, not a price negotiation.
perspective_data.what_I_saw_others_didnt: During the Suez blockage, everyone scrambled to find alternative shipping routes. I noticed that the real bottleneck was not the ships — it was customs clearance staffing at Mombasa port, which had been cut by forty percent during COVID and never restored. I rerouted two critical shipments through Dar es Salaam three days before Mombasa processing times tripled. That decision saved the company an estimated $340,000 in delay penalties.

judgment_data.decision: In September 2021, I terminated our largest supplier contract — a relationship that represented thirty-eight percent of our annual volume — because the supplier began requiring ninety-day advance payment instead of net-thirty terms.
judgment_data.stakes: Losing the supplier meant we had no backup for three critical product lines. Our sales team estimated a revenue gap of $1.2 million over six months. Two board members explicitly opposed the decision.
judgment_data.reasoning: The payment term change was not about cash flow — the supplier was preparing to exit the East African market entirely and was extracting maximum short-term value. Agreeing to their terms would have meant prepaying for inventory from a supplier who would stop servicing warranty claims within a year. I had seen this pattern before with a different supplier in 2019.
judgment_data.consequence: We found two smaller regional suppliers within eight weeks. Initial costs were fourteen percent higher, but both offered net-thirty terms and local warranty service. When the original supplier exited the market five months later, competitors who had stayed were left with unsupported inventory. Our revenue dip was $410,000 — roughly a third of the projected worst case.
judgment_data.learned: The most dangerous supplier is one who changes terms without explaining why. Payment terms are a leading indicator of strategic intent, not a negotiation tactic. I now treat any unexplained term change as a signal to investigate the supplier's broader business position before responding to the terms themselves.

interpretive_data.framework_emerged: Supplier Stress Index — an informal scoring model I developed that weights three factors: term stability over the last eight quarters, communication responsiveness during disruptions, and willingness to share their own supply chain risks transparently. Suppliers scoring below a threshold on any two factors get flagged for contingency planning regardless of their pricing.
interpretive_data.principle_crystallized: In emerging market logistics, the reliability of a relationship is more valuable than the efficiency of a transaction. Systems optimized purely for cost will fail at the exact moment cost matters most.
interpretive_data.transferable_pattern: When an established partner changes terms suddenly, the explanation they give is less important than the explanation they do not give. Unexplained behavioral shifts in long-term business relationships are almost always strategic — they reflect a decision already made, not a negotiation still open.

schema_gaps: Subject described a network of informal information-sharing relationships with competitors that informed early warning signals — no field captures inter-firm intelligence networks
Subject referenced cultural negotiation norms specific to East African business contexts that shaped their approach — no field captures culturally situated reasoning
